target.title: Fail Codex compaction at the Codex boundary
target.body:
## Summary

Codex runtime compaction was still being treated like an OpenClaw-owned PI safeguard in a few places. OpenClaw could preflight-compact Codex reply turns, post-turn compact Codex CLI transcripts, let a context-engine plugin replace Codex app-server compaction, and force plugin compaction before or after Codex `turn/start` overflow handling.

That is the wrong boundary. Codex already owns automatic compaction inside the Codex runtime. OpenClaw should not predict when a Codex thread needs compaction and then rewrite the transcript around it. At best that duplicates the native harness. At worst it crosses runtime/auth boundaries, restarts the shared app-server, or drifts a Codex-backed OpenAI session into OpenClaw/public Responses summarization.

This tightens the contract. Automatic compaction for Codex runtime sessions is native Codex-owned. OpenClaw now skips its preflight and CLI automatic compaction paths for Codex, removes the Codex app-server context-engine forced-compaction paths, and no longer lets an `ownsCompaction` context engine take over Codex app-server compaction. Explicit compaction is still supported: `/compact` and plugin-requested/manual compaction requests are forwarded to Codex as native `thread/compact/start`, and context-engine maintenance can run afterward when native compaction succeeds.

The fail-closed behavior from the earlier patch stays: if native Codex compaction cannot run or cannot complete, OpenClaw reports that failure honestly. It does not restart the shared Codex app-server as a retry strategy, and it does not fall back to OpenClaw/public OpenAI compaction for Codex runtime sessions. Non-Codex pinned harnesses still keep their previous context-engine fallback path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Codex-runtime sessions should not be automatically compacted by OpenClaw preflight, CLI, context-engine, or public OpenAI summarization paths. Explicit/manual/plugin compaction should still forward to native Codex, and native Codex compaction failures should fail closed.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout using the Codex app-server compaction seam, Codex app-server turn-start lifecycle, reply preflight compaction seam, CLI compaction lifecycle, embedded runner compaction seam, and docs/changelog checks.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts`; `git diff --check`; `pnpm check:changed`; `pnpm build`; `pnpm lint:docs`.

Evidence after fix: App-server tests now prove manual/plugin compaction forwards to native `thread/compact/start` even when a context engine advertises `ownsCompaction`, and missing native bindings fail instead of delegating to that engine. Reply preflight tests now prove Codex runtime sessions return unchanged even with over-threshold token snapshots. CLI tests now prove Codex runtime sessions skip OpenClaw automatic CLI compaction entirely, while non-Codex pinned harness fallback still works. Run-attempt lifecycle tests now prove Codex turn-start overflow recovery does not call plugin compaction or wait on a hung context-engine compact call.

Observed result after fix: Targeted tests, changed checks, formatting, docs lint, diff whitespace checks, and build all passed locally.

What was not tested: I did not force a destructive live compaction timeout on Pash's running dev agent session.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- start a local HTTP server serving test responses (HTML error, empty, valid script, escape attack). Step 2 -- extract the real `run_remote_bash` from the patched `install.sh` and call it against each endpoint:
```bash
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server**

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

**Sanitization detail**

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte):
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

**Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)**

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

**Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS**

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` and `validate_downloaded_script` functions reject HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. The HTTPS proof uses the real unmodified `download_file` function with `--proto '=https' --tlsv1.2` over TLS against a self-signed cert trusted via `CURL_CA_BUNDLE`. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).





---

## ClawSweeper review findings addressed (a898dce97d)

### [P2] Raw byte shebang check (scripts/install.sh)

**Before:** `first_line` was populated via command substitution (`head -c 256 | head -1`), so bash discarded NUL bytes before the `#!` prefix check. A file with leading NUL bytes before `#!` would false-accept.

**After:** Uses `od -An -tx1 -N2` to read the first two raw file bytes and compare against `2321` (hex for `#!`). The command-substitution path is now only used for the diagnostic message on rejection.

NUL-prefix bypass proof:
```console
$ printf '\x00\x00#!/bin/bash\necho pwned\n' > /tmp/nul_test.sh
$ od -An -tx1 -N2 /tmp/nul_test.sh | tr -d ' '
0000
# Raw bytes are 0x00 0x00, not 0x23 0x21 → rejected
```

### [P3] Portable control-byte assertion (scripts/test-install-validation.sh)

**Before:** `grep -P '[\x00-\x1f\x7f\x80-\x9f]'` (GNU-only, fails on macOS BSD grep).

**After:** `tr -d '\000-\037\177\200-\237'` + string comparison. Fully POSIX-portable.

### Updated harness output (13/13 pass, macOS)

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  C1 escape    → rejected
  ✓ PASS  NUL prefix   → rejected (raw byte check caught NUL before #!)
  ✓ PASS  C1 diagnostic → C1 bytes stripped from error output
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 13 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed — validation works as intended.
```

target.title: Guard OpenAI chat payload turns
target.body:
Fixes #86145

## Summary
- Refuse OpenAI-compatible chat streams when compaction/transport transforms leave a system/tool-only payload with no user or assistant turns.
- Classify blank prompt submissions with system/tool-only replay as empty history instead of replay-backed blank user prompts.
- Add regressions for the stream-time guard and compaction branch recovery of user/assistant turns when preparation only contains tool output.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Qwen/OpenAI-compatible chat requests no longer reach the provider with a `messages[]` payload that has no user or assistant turn after compaction and transport transforms.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw runtime on this PR branch, using `createOpenAICompletionsTransportStreamFn` against a real local HTTP/SSE endpoint that behaves like an OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` server.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with a script that first sends a positive-control user turn to the loopback OpenAI-compatible endpoint, then sends a system-only runtime prompt through the same stream function.

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output from the runtime proof run:

```json
{
  "proof": "PR #86177 OpenAI-compatible runtime guard",
  "runtime": "real local OpenAI-compatible HTTP/SSE endpoint via createOpenAICompletionsTransportStreamFn",
  "model": "mlx/mlx-community/Qwen3-30B-A3B-6bit",
  "endpoint": "http://127.0.0.1:[REDACTED_PORT]/v1",
  "positive_control": {
    "requests_received": 1,
    "captured_roles": ["system", "user"],
    "done_reason": "stop"
  },
  "system_only_guard": {
    "requests_before": 1,
    "requests_after": 1,
    "network_requests_delta": 0,
    "error_event_seen": true,
    "stop_reason": "error",
    "error_message": "OpenAI-compatible chat payload for mlx/mlx-community/Qwen3-30B-A3B-6bit contains no user or assistant messages after compaction and transport transforms; refusing to send a system/tool-only request. Start a new user turn or repair the compacted session history."
  },
  "result": "PASS: system-only OpenAI-compatible payload was caught locally before HTTP send"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The positive-control request reached the loopback endpoint and captured `messages` roles `["system", "user"]`, proving the runtime endpoint was live and normal OpenAI-compatible sends still reach the network. The system-only run emitted the new local error, and the loopback endpoint request count stayed unchanged (`network_requests_delta: 0`), showing the malformed payload was blocked locally before `chat.completions.create` sent an HTTP request to the endpoint.

What was not tested: No external paid or remote Qwen provider call was made. This proof uses a real local OpenAI-compatible HTTP/SSE endpoint to show the transport guard fires before network send; maintainers should still decide whether the user/assistant-free payload invariant should remain global or be provider-compat scoped.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

If this PR is squashed or reworked, please preserve Andy Ye as the author or include:
Co-authored-by: Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com>

target.title: fix(agents): strip plaintext model provider keys
target.body:
## Summary
- strip plaintext provider `apiKey` values after merge-mode preservation, source-managed enforcement, and provider compatibility transforms so old `models.json` keys cannot be reintroduced into the final prompt-facing catalog
- clean legacy catalog-only `models.json` plaintext provider keys before the empty-provider `skip` path can preserve them
- migrate existing `models.json`-only plaintext provider keys into the target agent's local `auth-profiles.json` before writing the stripped catalog when there is no configured provider key and no target-local auth profile for that provider
- keep inherited main-agent profiles and runtime/external overlays from suppressing local legacy `models.json` key migration for non-main agents
- migrate known/env-present legacy env-name catalog credentials such as `LITELLM_KEY` to env-backed auth-profile `keyRef`s, while preserving ambiguous env-shaped plaintext strings as inline local auth-profile keys
- preserve non-secret markers such as env SecretRef markers and custom-provider `secretref-managed` compatibility markers
- add regression coverage for generated plaintext stripping, merge-mode stripping, catalog-only cleanup, existing-key migration, fail-closed behavior, inherited-profile migration boundaries, env-present keyRef migration, ambiguous env-shaped key preservation, and pi model discovery compatibility

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated and legacy `models.json` files should not persist plaintext provider `apiKey` values. Cleanup should preserve upgrade auth outside the prompt-facing catalog, including non-main agent local migration boundaries and env-shaped legacy values that must only become `keyRef`s when an env marker or observed env value proves they are env-backed.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source from this PR branch at `b23d1d7c`, macOS, Node.js `v24.1.0`, `/usr/bin/python3` for fs-safe pinned writes. Provider keys in proof output are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
FS_SAFE_PYTHON_MODE=require FS_SAFE_PYTHON=/usr/bin/python3 node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The inline Node proof script created temporary local agent dirs, called `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, `discoverModels`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `createProviderAuthResolver`, and exercised these real paths: inline custom provider stripping, legacy configured `models.json` key migration, catalog-only cleanup, env-present `keyRef` migration, ambiguous env-shaped plaintext preservation, non-main inherited-profile migration boundary, and pi model discovery compatibility.

- Evidence after fix (copied live output, redacted):

```json
{
  "head": "b23d1d7c",
  "tempRoot": "<tmp>",
  "inlineCustomProvider": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "discoverModelsFindsCustomModel": "custom-model"
  },
  "legacyModelsJsonOnlyUpgrade": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsLegacyModel": "legacy-model"
  },
  "catalogOnlyLegacyCleanup": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsCatalogOnlyModel": "catalog-only-model"
  },
  "envPresentCatalogMigration": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": false,
      "keyRef": {
        "source": "env",
        "provider": "default",
        "id": "LITELLM_KEY"
      },
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "apiKeyMarker": "LITELLM_KEY",
      "resolvedEnvValueSeen": true
    }
  },
  "ambiguousEnvShapedCredential": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": true,
      "hasKeyRef": false,
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "preservedLocalKeySeen": true
    }
  },
  "inheritedProfileBoundary": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "localMigratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "resolvedWorkerKey": true
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the final `models.json` omits plaintext keys; non-built-in custom providers with inline model rows retain the non-secret `secretref-managed` marker; old configured and catalog-only `models.json` credentials migrate into `auth-profiles.json`; env-present legacy catalog values migrate as env-backed `keyRef` credentials and resolve through provider auth; ambiguous env-shaped legacy values with no env signal are preserved as inline local auth-profile keys instead of unresolved env refs; a non-main agent with an inherited same-profile ID still migrates its local legacy catalog key and runtime auth resolves the worker-local key; and pi `discoverModels` can still find custom model rows.
- What was not tested: I did not test a real paid third-party provider. The live proof did not force auth-profile persistence failure; that fail-closed path is covered by the focused regression test listed below.

## Tests

target.title: fix(auto-reply): strip reasoning blocks before NO_REPLY detection (#66701)
target.body:
## Summary

`isSilentReplyText` in `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` uses an exact-match regex (`^\s*NO_REPLY\s*$`) to suppress agent replies. Some models can prepend reasoning blocks before the token:

```text
<think>
Cav is talking to someone else. I will stay quiet here.
</think>NO_REPLY
```

The regex fails on this input and the raw reasoning text is posted to chat.

This patch calls `stripReasoningTagsFromText` from `src/shared/text/reasoning-tags.ts` before testing the input. That strips supported reasoning tags such as `<think>`, `<thinking>`, and `<antthinking>` before the exact `NO_REPLY` match.

The #19537 behavior is covered by tests: substantive replies that end with `NO_REPLY` are still not suppressed after reasoning-tag stripping.

Closes #66701.

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` — import `stripReasoningTagsFromText` and strip reasoning tags before the exact-match check in `isSilentReplyText`
- `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts` — 7 new test cases for reasoning-prefix variants, multiple blocks, token-inside-block, and unclosed-block cases

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `isSilentReplyText("<think>I will stay quiet.</think>NO_REPLY")` returned `false`, which allowed reasoning text to be posted to chat; it now returns `true`.

Real environment tested: unit test suite `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts`

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

Evidence after fix:
```text
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true when <think> block precedes token (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true when <think> block with whitespace precedes token (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true for <antthinking> block prefix (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns false when substantive text follows token after reasoning strip (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true for multiple sequential reasoning blocks before token (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns false when token is inside a reasoning block with content after (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns false for unclosed reasoning block containing substantive text before token (#66701)
Tests: 31 passed (31)
```

Observed result after fix: all 31 tests pass; the 7 new #66701 cases cover the pure-function fix surface.

What was not tested: live Telegram, Signal, or Slack delivery with a real Gemini or Haiku model producing a reasoning-prefixed `NO_REPLY`. Crabbox proof would cover that full path.

target.title: Doctor: structure shell completion repair
target.body:
## Summary

This PR moves shell completion doctor repair onto the internal runnable health-check path while keeping the existing public health-check surface normalized.

The rule remains one flow: check shell completion status, return lint findings when repair is false, and run the existing repair actions when repair is true. `doctorShellCompletion()` stays as the positional presentation wrapper and now prints the structured warnings/changes returned by that flow.

Key behavior:

- `openclaw doctor --lint` can report slow dynamic completion profiles and missing completion cache as structured findings.
- `openclaw doctor --fix` still repairs stale dynamic completion profiles and missing caches from the shell-completion position in doctor order.
- Optional missing shell completion remains quiet in lint/non-interactive detection and still prompts only in repair mode.
- Runnable checks are normalized at registry boundaries so public `openclaw/plugin-sdk/health` list/get consumers still receive detect-capable registered checks.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: shell completion doctor repair now participates in structured doctor lint/repair without exposing a public run-only SDK contract.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu 24.04 worktree rebased on latest `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `git diff --check`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/health-check-registry.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/flows/health-check-registry.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-browser-residue-check.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
- `bash /tmp/codex-review --mode branch`

Evidence after fix: focused Vitest passed 6 files / 56 tests; core tsgo and core test tsgo both passed; codex-review reported no actionable correctness issues.

Observed result after fix: shell completion stale-cache and slow-dynamic cases are structured findings/repairs; optional install remains prompt-only in repair mode; public registry entries remain normalized.

What was not tested: live mutation of a real user shell profile.

## Summary

- add a shared strict positive-integer Commander option parser
- use it for `plugins search --limit` and `skills search --limit`
- cover the helper and both CLI parse paths so invalid limits stop before ClawHub calls

Fixes #84473.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `plugins search --limit` and `skills search --limit` no longer accept partially numeric values such as `1abc`; they fail during CLI option parsing before calling ClawHub.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, branch `fix/84473-search-limit-parser`, commit `b2c536f8cd55e0cd7f61b916cc0c23a243460a04`, Node v24.11.1.

- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the local source checkout:

```console
$ node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins search calendar --limit 1abc --json
OpenClaw could not parse this command: option '--limit <n>' argument '1abc' is invalid. Expected a positive integer.
Try: openclaw plugins search --help
```

```console
$ node --import tsx src/entry.ts skills search calendar --limit 1abc --json
OpenClaw could not parse this command: option '--limit <n>' argument '1abc' is invalid. Expected a positive integer.
Try: openclaw skills search --help
```

Expected a positive integer.`, and `--limit -5` prints the same error for `-5`, all before any ClawHub call.

## Validation

commands.author: pashpashpash

target.title: Fail Codex compaction at the Codex boundary
target.body:
## Summary

Codex runtime compaction was still being treated like an OpenClaw-owned PI safeguard in a few places. OpenClaw could preflight-compact Codex reply turns, post-turn compact Codex CLI transcripts, let a context-engine plugin replace Codex app-server compaction, and force plugin compaction before or after Codex `turn/start` overflow handling.

That is the wrong boundary. Codex already owns automatic compaction inside the Codex runtime. OpenClaw should not predict when a Codex thread needs compaction and then rewrite the transcript around it. At best that duplicates the native harness. At worst it crosses runtime/auth boundaries, restarts the shared app-server, or drifts a Codex-backed OpenAI session into OpenClaw/public Responses summarization.

This tightens the contract. Automatic compaction for Codex runtime sessions is native Codex-owned. OpenClaw now skips its preflight and CLI automatic compaction paths for Codex, removes the Codex app-server context-engine forced-compaction paths, and no longer lets an `ownsCompaction` context engine take over Codex app-server compaction. Explicit compaction is still supported: `/compact` and plugin-requested/manual compaction requests are forwarded to Codex as native `thread/compact/start`, and context-engine maintenance can run afterward when native compaction succeeds.

The fail-closed behavior from the earlier patch stays: if native Codex compaction cannot run or cannot complete, OpenClaw reports that failure honestly. It does not restart the shared Codex app-server as a retry strategy, and it does not fall back to OpenClaw/public OpenAI compaction for Codex runtime sessions. Non-Codex pinned harnesses still keep their previous context-engine fallback path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Codex-runtime sessions should not be automatically compacted by OpenClaw preflight, CLI, context-engine, or public OpenAI summarization paths. Explicit/manual/plugin compaction should still forward to native Codex, and native Codex compaction failures should fail closed.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout using the Codex app-server compaction seam, Codex app-server turn-start lifecycle, reply preflight compaction seam, CLI compaction lifecycle, embedded runner compaction seam, and docs/changelog checks.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts`; `git diff --check`; `pnpm check:changed`; `pnpm build`; `pnpm lint:docs`.

Evidence after fix: App-server tests now prove manual/plugin compaction forwards to native `thread/compact/start` even when a context engine advertises `ownsCompaction`, and missing native bindings fail instead of delegating to that engine. Reply preflight tests now prove Codex runtime sessions return unchanged even with over-threshold token snapshots. CLI tests now prove Codex runtime sessions skip OpenClaw automatic CLI compaction entirely, while non-Codex pinned harness fallback still works. Run-attempt lifecycle tests now prove Codex turn-start overflow recovery does not call plugin compaction or wait on a hung context-engine compact call.

Observed result after fix: Targeted tests, changed checks, formatting, docs lint, diff whitespace checks, and build all passed locally.

What was not tested: I did not force a destructive live compaction timeout on Pash's running dev agent session.

target.title: feat(memory): add multi-slot memory role architecture
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Replaces the single scalar `plugins.slots.memory` selection model with purpose-specific memory role slots so memory plugins can compose instead of displacing one another.
- Adds first-class config/schema/type/docs support for `memory.recall`, `memory.compaction`, `memory.capture`, `memory.dreaming`, and `memory.userModel`, while keeping legacy `plugins.slots.memory` as a backward-compatible recall shorthand.
- Adds per-agent memory role-slot overrides under `agents.list[*].plugins.slots` so new memory providers can be tested or scoped to one agent without changing the global memory stack.
- Wires the role-slot model through plugin activation, gateway startup preloading, runtime memory resolution, skill publishing, config validation, doctor repair/preview surfaces, uninstall cleanup, status diagnostics, and docs.

Why does this matter now?

- #60572 documents repeated production pressure from memory-core, LosslessClaw, Hindsight, Honcho, Mem0, ByteRover, and memory-core dreaming all solving different memory problems while competing for one global slot.
- The old slot contract forced useful tools into workarounds: context-engine ownership, sidecars, or global memory replacement.
- The branch makes the platform contract match the layered memory architecture users are already trying to operate.

What is the intended outcome?

- `memory-core` can remain the factual recall owner while other plugins own capture, compaction, dreaming, or user-model roles.
- Existing configs using `plugins.slots.memory` continue to work and migrate/normalize toward `plugins.slots["memory.recall"]` without a flag day.
- Plugin lifecycle and diagnostics treat role-slot-selected memory plugins as intentionally active across startup, validation, skill publishing, and cleanup.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This does not replace `plugins.slots.contextEngine`; LosslessClaw and other context engines continue to use the existing `contextEngine` slot.
- This does not implement the owner-provenance object shape proposed in #70823; slot values remain scalar plugin IDs for this PR.
- This does not add agent-scoped `contextEngine` semantics.
- This does not force third-party plugins to adopt every role immediately; existing memory plugins still default to recall-compatible behavior.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Backward compatibility for legacy `plugins.slots.memory` configs and `doctor --fix` migration behavior.
- Whether role-slot-selected plugins are consistently considered active across loader, gateway startup, validation, skills, uninstall, and diagnostics.
- Per-agent override behavior, especially restrictive plugin allowlists and explicit plugin disable/deny cases.
- Preservation of context-engine behavior for LosslessClaw-style plugins.

## Linked context

Closes #60572.

Related #70823 for future machine-readable slot ownership/provenance. This PR intentionally keeps the scalar slot-id model and solves the memory role composition layer first.

Related historical/runtime context gathered during analysis:

- #78540: active memory plugin diagnostics could misreport configured memory providers.
- #76550: stale `plugins.slots.*` entries could leave config unrecoverable until doctor cleanup was broadened.
- #77063, #77185, #76576, #81843: context-engine registration and LosslessClaw-adjacent failures shaped the compatibility review for preserving `plugins.slots.contextEngine` behavior.
- #77714: persistent/pluggable memory backend demand is part of the same ecosystem pressure.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: memory plugins no longer have to globally displace each other to participate in distinct memory roles. The active memory/runtime/plugin surfaces now understand role-slot selections globally and per agent.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Dev-01, branch `feat/multi-slot-memory-architecture`, using the repository Node/pnpm toolchain.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:

```sh
corepack pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts config schema | jq '.properties.plugins.properties.slots.properties | keys'
corepack pnpm tsgo:core
corepack pnpm oxfmt --check ...
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...
```

Evidence after fix:

Terminal output from the real source CLI on this branch:

```text
$ corepack pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts config schema | jq '.properties.plugins.properties.slots.properties | keys'
[
  "contextEngine",
  "memory",
  "memory.capture",
  "memory.compaction",
  "memory.dreaming",
  "memory.recall",
  "memory.userModel"
]
```

Supplemental deterministic validation:

- Focused branch batch: 6 files, 349 tests passed.
- Broader role-slot batch: 11 files, 313 tests passed.
- Branch-risk regression batch: 8 files, 378 tests passed.
- Additional focused batches: 154 tests and 62 tests passed.
- Earlier context-engine compatibility batch: 342 tests passed across plugin slot, context-engine, config, and doctor warning coverage.

Observed result after fix:

- The source CLI schema exposes `memory.recall`, `memory.compaction`, `memory.capture`, `memory.dreaming`, and `memory.userModel` alongside legacy `memory` and `contextEngine`.
- `plugins.slots.memory` remains accepted as legacy recall shorthand.
- `plugins.slots["memory.recall"]` canonicalizes recall selection.
- Per-agent `agents.list[*].plugins.slots[...]` memory overrides validate and participate in plugin activation where intended.
- Role-slot-selected memory plugins are included in startup plugin IDs, loader activation, plugin skill publishing, config validation, status/doctor surfaces, and uninstall/stale-slot repair paths.
- Context-engine slot behavior remains independent; `plugins.slots.contextEngine = "lossless-claw"` is still resolved through the existing context-engine path.

What was not tested:

- A live third-party LosslessClaw install was not run from this branch.
- Future owner-provenance object semantics from #70823 are not implemented or tested here.

Proof limitations or environment constraints:

- Validation was local and focused on deterministic branch risk. Broad project gates should be covered by CI after this PR opens.
- Unit tests, typechecks, lint, and CI are supplemental to the copied CLI output above.

## Implementation notes

- Adds `src/plugins/slot-resolution.ts` as the shared role-slot helper layer for canonical memory slot keys, defaults, normalization, and configured selection collection.
- Updates config schema/types/help/labels/docs for global and per-agent memory role slots.
- Migrates legacy memory slot handling through doctor repair sequencing and preview warnings.
- Updates plugin loader/activation/startup resolution so explicit role-slot selections activate memory plugins without confusing default `memory-core` fallback with a user-selected plugin under restrictive allowlists.
- Extends validation to report missing role-slot plugin refs and validate schemas for plugins selected only by non-recall or per-agent memory roles.
- Extends skill publishing, status scanning, uninstall cleanup, memory runtime, dreaming, compaction provider, and SDK alias behavior to use the role-aware model.
- Updates bundled memory plugin manifests/docs to describe role support and the recall shorthand migration.

## Compatibility and risk

Backward compatibility preserved:

- Existing `plugins.slots.memory` configs continue to resolve as recall selection.
- The default recall plugin remains `memory-core` unless explicitly disabled or overridden.
- `plugins.enabled = false`, denylist behavior, and explicit `plugins.entries.<id>.enabled = false` remain respected.
- `plugins.slots.contextEngine` remains separate from memory roles and continues to select context engines such as LosslessClaw.

Primary risks:

- Third-party plugins that read `plugins.slots.memory` directly may need to learn the canonical `memory.recall` key for best UX, although the legacy shorthand remains supported.
- The public memory role taxonomy becomes durable API surface after merge, so naming and docs deserve careful maintainer review.
- Per-agent memory role overrides are intentionally limited to memory roles; extending other slots later should be a separate product/API decision.

## Verification

- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm oxfmt --check ...`
- `git diff --check`
- Focused branch regression batches totaling more than 1,500 passing test cases across plugin slots, loader activation, config validation, startup plugin IDs, memory runtime, doctor repair, status scanning, plugin skills, uninstall cleanup, dreaming, context-engine compatibility, and related config surfaces.

CI should run the full project gate for final merge confidence.

target.title: fix(gateway): clear admin scopes for backend self-pairing
target.body:
Summary
- Stop treating shared token/password auth as sufficient to preserve any self-declared operator scopes for local backend self-pairing.
- Keep unscoped backend local handoff intact, while routing scoped local backend Gateway calls through normal device identity so internal loopback calls still work.
- Add explicit issue coverage for loopback/no-Origin backend identity + shared token/password + self-declared operator scopes + no device identity.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Device-less local/shared-secret backend Gateway clients must not preserve self-declared operator scopes while skipping pairing.

**Real environment tested:** Temporary local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR branch, token auth, loopback WebSocket at `ws://127.0.0.1:39139`, channels skipped, configured via a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started the local Gateway from this branch, then ran a Node/tsx WebSocket client using `GatewayClient` as `gateway-client` / `backend` with shared token auth, `deviceIdentity: null`, and `scopes: ["operator.write"]`. The client then attempted `sessions.list`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live local Gateway client:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-... \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-.../openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=39139 \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=secret \
  node --import tsx --input-type=module

local gateway: ws://127.0.0.1:39139
backend shared-token no-device requested scopes: [operator.write]
hello auth scopes: []
sessions.list error: missing scope: operator.read
```

Gateway runtime log excerpt for the same live connection:

```text
[ws] ⇄ res ✗ sessions.list 1ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST errorMessage=missing scope: operator.read
```

**Observed result after fix:** The backend shared-token, no-device connection completed, but the server returned no authorized scopes in `hello-ok`; the follow-up read-scoped RPC failed with `missing scope: operator.read`. This verifies that non-admin self-declared scopes are cleared too, not only `operator.admin`.

**What was not tested:** Remote browser-origin behavior was not live-tested here; focused regression coverage covers the local and shared-secret-loopback cases changed by this PR.

Additional verification
```text
$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.compat-baseline.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts

Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  165 passed (165)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server.sessions-send.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/code-mode.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  30 passed (30)

$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#72418

target.title: fix(telegram): transient Telegram pairing prompts
target.body: ## Summary The behavior I'm seeing is transient Telegram pairing requests after an idle: BEFORE After a deep dive this is most likely caused by FD pressure after returning from idle - I've ONLY experienced on return from idle. In that case, I think it should error not send a confusing and potentially time-consuming pairing request. This replication was done with a chmod 800 to have same effect. AFTER fail loudly, better than spurious pairing request. I've added a comment in code to consider backoff retries to wait till FD pressure lowers, but feels a bit edgecasey and clever for now. ## Solution - Avoid treating transient Telegram pairing-store read failures as an empty pairing allowlist that triggers a new pairing challenge. - Skip pairing-store reads when the effective DM policy is not pairing, or when configured `allowFrom` already authorizes the sender. - Preserve per-DM pairing policy resolution when flushing long text fragments. ## Verification - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> 91 passed - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts` -> 5 passed - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> passed - `git diff --check` -> passed - `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings Behavior addressed: Same private Telegram chat can receive a transient pairing-store read failure without getting a bogus pairing prompt, then respond normally once the next store read succeeds. Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on Node/Vitest with mocked Telegram bot handlers and pairing-store dependency failures. Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the two focused Telegram test files above, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and autoreview. Evidence after fix: `bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` passes 91 tests, including the same-chat back-to-back message regression where the first store read rejects and the second returns the paired sender id. Observed result after fix: No pairing request is upserted on transient store failure; configured/open DMs avoid unnecessary store reads; per-DM pairing store authorization still works for text-fragment flushes. What was not tested: No live Telegram round trip was run; this is covered with mocked bot handler regressions rather than Crabbox/live Telegram proof.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2D Slack migration proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Slack DMs and channel threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Slack remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `aa278f12eb` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `aa278f12eb`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR. - Includes a tiny test-isolation hardening for `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally.

target.title: fix: tolerate Bun launcher import misses
target.body:
## Summary
- match Bun direct module-miss messages for optional launcher imports that omit ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND
- keep direct-miss matching scoped to the requested specifier or resolved path
- add regression coverage for Bun-style launcher module-not-found messages

Fixes #86198

## Tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/openclaw-launcher-module-not-found.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Bun packaged launcher startup no longer exits when optional `./dist/warning-filter.js` / `.mjs` imports are absent and Bun reports the direct import miss without `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND`.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux x64 checkout with Bun v1.3.13, using a temp package directory containing this patched `openclaw.mjs`, minimal package metadata, and a dummy packaged `dist/entry.js`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temp package directory, copied the patched launcher, wrote a minimal `package.json`, wrote `dist/entry.js`, intentionally did not create `dist/warning-filter.js` or `dist/warning-filter.mjs`, then launched it with Bun.
- Evidence after fix: Console output from the temp packaged-launcher smoke:

```text
$ tmp=$(mktemp -d) && cp openclaw.mjs "$tmp/openclaw.mjs" && printf '{"version":"test","type":"module"}\n' > "$tmp/package.json" && mkdir -p "$tmp/dist" && printf 'console.log("entry loaded")\n' > "$tmp/dist/entry.js" && (cd "$tmp" && bun ./openclaw.mjs)
entry loaded
```

- Observed result after fix: The launcher skipped the missing optional warning-filter module and loaded `dist/entry.js` successfully, printing `entry loaded`; it did not throw `Cannot find module './dist/warning-filter.js'`.
- What was not tested: Full published package install under Bun; this was a focused packaged-launcher smoke for the reported missing optional warning-filter import path.

target.title: Preserve TUI optimistic user messages across history reloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve optimistic outbound TUI user messages as pending chat-log entries instead of plain rendered entries.
- Keep pending user messages across `chat.history` rebuilds, reconcile them once persisted history catches up, and clear only the failed pending send on `chat.send` errors.
- Add focused TUI regression coverage plus an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Related Issues

Refs #54722 and #59014 for the earlier closed TUI user-message disappearance regressions. Related but distinct from #78017, which tracks long-message scrollback loss in the underlying renderer.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: TUI user messages no longer disappear when a stale history reload or run-error refresh happens before the optimistic outbound message is present in persisted history.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, TUI unit config and fake-backend PTY harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix: focused TUI tests passed 4 files / 115 tests; fake-backend PTY lane passed 1 file / 11 tests; `git diff --check` passed; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.

Observed result after fix: optimistic user messages are kept through stale history rebuilds and committed/reconciled once the real run or persisted history catches up.

What was not tested: no live Gateway/provider run was executed; the fake-backend PTY harness does not prove Gateway transport, embedded runtime, providers, session persistence, or live streaming.

target.title: [AI-assisted] Add ClickClack slash command suggestions
target.body:
## Summary
- enrich the shared native command registry with usage/menu metadata for slash command autocomplete
- add a gateway-authenticated ClickClack plugin route at `POST /clickclack/commands/suggest` for composer slash command suggestions
- filter suggestions through ClickClack account allowlists plus `commands.allowFrom` and `commands.ownerAllowFrom`
- document the backend response shape for ClickClack UI integrations

## Real behavior proof
Setup: local OpenClaw checkout on branch `codex/clickclack-slash-commands`, using a temporary OpenClaw home with dummy/redacted ClickClack config and a throwaway gateway on `127.0.0.1:18799`. No real ClickClack token, real user id, or production gateway auth token was used in this proof.

Temporary proof config used these redacted values:

```json
{
  "gateway": { "mode": "local", "auth": { "mode": "token", "token": "proof-token-redacted" } },
  "commands": {
    "allowFrom": { "clickclack": ["usr_local"] },
    "ownerAllowFrom": ["clickclack:usr_owner"]
  },
  "channels": {
    "clickclack": {
      "enabled": true,
      "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:8080",
      "token": "ccb_proof_redacted",
      "workspace": "wsp_local",
      "defaultTo": "channel:general",
      "allowFrom": ["usr_local"],
      "replyMode": "agent"
    }
  }
}
```

Commands run after the patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr86203-proof HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr86203-proof \
  node dist/index.js gateway --port 18799

body='{"accountId":"default","query":"/st","senderId":"usr_local","channelId":"chn_general","channelName":"general","limit":5}'

curl -sS -o /tmp/pr86203-unauth.json -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18799/clickclack/commands/suggest \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  --data "$body"

curl -sS -o /tmp/pr86203-auth.json -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18799/clickclack/commands/suggest \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer proof-token-redacted' \
  --data "$body"

restart_body='{"accountId":"default","query":"/restart","senderId":"usr_local","channelId":"chn_general","channelName":"general","limit":5}'

curl -sS -o /tmp/pr86203-restart.json -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18799/clickclack/commands/suggest \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer proof-token-redacted' \
  --data "$restart_body"
```

Observed result:

```text
unauth_status=401
auth_status=200
auth_restart_status=200
```

Parsed response summary:

```json
{
  "unauth": {
    "suggestions": [],
    "error": { "message": "Unauthorized", "type": "unauthorized" }
  },
  "authSlashSt": {
    "query": "/st",
    "suggestions": ["/status", "/stop", "/steer", "/crestodian", "/fast"]
  },
  "authRestartAsNonOwner": {
    "query": "/restart",
    "suggestions": [],
    "emptyText": "No matching OpenClaw command."
  }
}
```

This verifies the running gateway route now requires gateway auth, returns normal slash suggestions for an authorized ClickClack sender, and does not suggest the owner-only `/restart` command to a non-owner sender.

Not tested yet: a browser-level ClickClack UI autocomplete flow. This PR is still draft until feature acceptance/maintainer direction is confirmed.

## Validation
- `pnpm test:extension clickclack` (5 files, 22 tests passed)
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...` on changed files
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- `pnpm build`

## Validation notes
- `pnpm test:contracts:channels` initially failed on stale `node_modules/.experimental-vitest-cache` temp-file ENOENT. After moving that cache aside, it reran and failed on existing `qa-channel` contract metadata: `expected undefined to be 'qa-channel'` for `plugin.meta.id`. That failure is outside ClickClack and does not appear caused by this PR.
- A broad focused Vitest invocation against individual ClickClack/command tests hit the known local ClickClack plugin-timing spin; `pnpm test:extension clickclack` is the successful extension lane used instead.
- `codex review --base origin/main` was attempted but local Codex auth failed with Responses API `401 Unauthorized` / missing bearer auth, so no Codex review findings are available yet.

## AI-assisted disclosure
This PR was prepared with Codex assistance. I understand the code paths changed here: shared command metadata now exposes usage/menu data for autocomplete, and ClickClack has a gateway-authenticated backend suggestion resolver/HTTP route that filters suggestions through sender and owner authorization boundaries before returning command metadata.

target.title: Fix TUI Esc abort stale busy state
target.body:
## Summary

- Clear `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` after a successful TUI Esc abort, alongside `pendingChatRunId`.
- Track submitted prompts as TUI pending drafts keyed by the generated run id so early Esc abort can redact the pending transcript row and restore the text into the editor before gateway registration.
- Preserve/reconcile pending user rows across history reloads, and clear the draft-restore candidate as soon as chat/lifecycle/history proves the run is registered or durable.

Fixes #86199

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: TUI Esc abort could clear the pending run id while leaving `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` set, causing the next prompt to be blocked as busy even though a second Esc reported `no active run`. During the earliest submit window, a same-session history reload could also erase the just-submitted prompt before the run id was fully bound. Early Esc abort now restores the still-unregistered prompt to the editor instead of leaving it as a lost transcript row.

Real environment tested: macOS arm64, Node v22.22.0, OpenClaw worktree `tui-contradicting-esc-pattern`, commit 21f2ff2ccd.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
Test Files  5 passed (5)
     Tests  168 passed (168)
```

Observed result after fix: the regression coverage verifies that aborting a pending run clears both local busy-state fields, pending user prompts survive same-session history reloads when history has not committed them yet, pending prompts reconcile to a single history-backed row once the submitted prompt appears in history, and chat/lifecycle registration clears the local draft-restore candidate.

What was not tested: no live interactive TUI PTY run was executed; coverage is focused unit coverage for the state transitions behind the stale busy lock, early prompt disappearance, and early-abort draft restoration.

target.title: Fix local embedding worker safety
target.body:
## Summary

- Isolate local memory embeddings in a worker process by default so native `node-llama-cpp` teardown failures do not take down the gateway.
- Keep local embedding configuration unchanged; this PR does not add a `memorySearch.local.gpu` key or a QMD CPU policy knob.
- Degrade safely on local worker failure, activate configured embedding fallback with a reindex, and keep FTS-only search available when no fallback is configured.
- Keep worker-failure helpers internal to memory-core instead of expanding the public plugin SDK surface.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-timeout.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts` passed, 3 files / 44 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-timeout.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/search-manager.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.mistral-provider.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-targeted-sync.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` passed on rerun except one packed-run timeout in `search-manager.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/search-manager.test.ts` then passed, 33/33.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-44202-local-embedding-safety.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-44202-local-embedding-safety.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/plugin-sdk-dts-44202-local-embedding-safety.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Built bundle scan confirmed `dist/memory-core-local-embedding-worker.js` exists, the worker filters `--input-type`, no `gpuLayers`, `QMD_FORCE_CPU`, or local GPU policy names are emitted, and child close handling does not call `process.disconnect` after close ack.
- `PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` still fails locally because this checkout regenerates `@openclaw/fs-safe` source paths differently under `node_modules`; comparing generated current vs generated `origin/main` showed only that existing path-normalization difference and no memory/embedding API surface diff.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Apple Silicon local embedding worker crash risk from native `node-llama-cpp` / Metal cleanup is isolated from the gateway process without adding a new public config value.
Real environment tested: Apple Silicon macOS 26.4 arm64, Node 24.16.0; temporary installed `node-llama-cpp` package with the macOS arm64 Metal optional package present; local `ollama` was present but not part of this crash path.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: copied built `dist/` into `/tmp/openclaw-local-embedding-smoke`, installed `node-llama-cpp` there, imported the built embeddings chunk, and ran `createLocalEmbeddingProvider()` through the default worker sidecar using `hf:ggml-org/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-q8_0-GGUF/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf`, then called `embedQuery()` and `close()`.
Evidence after fix: the Apple Silicon smoke returned `provider=local`, `dimensions=768`, `magnitude=1`, `elapsedMs=457`; the parent process survived worker startup/use/close. Focused tests also simulate worker exit code 134 and prove structured degradation/fallback/FTS behavior.
Observed result after fix: local embeddings default to worker isolation; configured fallback activates and reindexes after a search-time local worker crash; no fallback keeps keyword search available; `memorySearch.local.gpu` is rejected by config validation; public plugin SDK exports do not include local-worker-only failure helpers.
What was not tested: the exact reported native Metal teardown crash was not reproduced locally; LM Studio and vLLM were not on PATH and are not part of the `node-llama-cpp` Metal cleanup path fixed here.

Fixes #44202

target.title: fix(agents): count exec-path openclaw cron add in successfulCronAdds
target.body:
### What

This PR fixes an issue where the `successfulCronAdds` counter was not incremented when an agent ran `openclaw cron add` as a shell command via `exec` or `bash`.

### Why

The existing logic only checked for the structured `cron` tool. This caused the agent runner to misread the counter and incorrectly append "I did not schedule a reminder" to the transcript after a successful shell-based cron addition.

### How

An `else if` block is added to `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` to inspect `exec`/`bash` results. It uses an anchored regex (`/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i`) to identify the command and requires a success receipt (`created|scheduled`) in the output before incrementing the counter.

This approach incorporates the hardened regex requested in the prior (self-closed) PR #53024. New regression tests are included to cover the fix and prevent bypasses.

Closes #52972

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Agent runner incorrectly appends "I did not schedule a reminder" after the agent runs `openclaw cron add` via exec/bash shell tool (structured `cron` tool path was unaffected).

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw development clone, Node 24, Linux (WSL2). Exec/bash tool path exercised via the handler directly.

**Exact steps or command run after fix:**
1. Agent issued `openclaw cron add "daily standup" "09:00"` via exec tool.
2. Exec result contained `Reminder created` in the aggregated output.
3. Inspected `ctx.state.successfulCronAdds` after handler returned.
4. Checked whether the "I did not schedule a reminder" note was appended to the transcript.

**Evidence after fix:**

Before fix — `successfulCronAdds` stays at 0 after a successful exec-path `openclaw cron add`, triggering the false "did not schedule" note.

After fix — handler reads the exec result, matches `/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i` on the command, finds `created` in the aggregated output, and increments the counter. Bypass attempt (`echo "openclaw cron add ..."`) correctly stays at 0 because the anchored regex rejects substring matches.

Console output (branch-local run):
```
✓ increments successfulCronAdds when exec runs openclaw cron add and output confirms creation
✓ does not increment successfulCronAdds for a bypass attempt (substring match without anchor)
✓ does not increment successfulCronAdds when openclaw cron add output lacks creation receipt
✓ does not increment successfulCronAdds for a cron add command without the openclaw prefix
  24 passed
```

**Observed result after fix:** Counter increments on valid `openclaw cron add` via exec; false "I did not schedule a reminder" note is suppressed. Counter stays 0 on bypass attempts, failed runs, and unrelated commands.

**What was not tested:** Live OpenClaw server with real cron backend and actual scheduled jobs; agents using non-standard shell wrappers around `openclaw cron add`; exec timeout paths.
