target.title: fix(doctor): preserve runtime pins during codex route repair [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor --fix` can rewrite `openai-codex/*` routes to canonical `openai/*` while replacing an explicit non-default `agentRuntime` pin, such as `pi`, with `codex`.
- Solution: carry the pre-repair runtime pin through the route migration and write a narrow canonical model-scoped runtime policy when needed.
- What changed: preserve explicit runtime pins from legacy agent model maps, provider-level runtime policy, and provider catalog model policy; add regression coverage for those cases and for listed-agent shielding.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not investigate or claim to fix the separate `gpt-5.5` token-usage behavior discussed on #84038, and it does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

## Motivation

#84362 is the ClawSweeper replacement for #84142, but that bot-owned branch is currently blocked by a `CHANGELOG.md` conflict. This PR provides the same fix path on current `upstream/main`, deliberately avoiding the contributor-forbidden changelog edit, and includes the tested follow-up runtime-pin cases.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #84038
- Related #84142
- Related #84362
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor --fix` route repair preserves explicit non-default runtime pins when migrating `openai-codex/gpt-5.4` to `openai/gpt-5.4`.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, Node v22.22.1, current `upstream/main` branch with this patch applied.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_REPO=/tmp/openclaw-84038-clean-20260521 pnpm exec tsx /tmp/repro-openclaw-84038-runtime-pin-matrix.mjs
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal output from the runtime resolver / doctor repair matrix:

```text
scenario: model-map PI pin survives legacy openai-codex model map rewrite
beforeRuntime: pi
defaultPolicy: { id: pi }
afterRuntime: pi
pass: true

scenario: provider-level PI pin survives route repair
beforeRuntime: pi
defaultPolicy: { id: pi }
afterRuntime: pi
pass: true

scenario: provider-catalog model PI pin survives route repair
beforeRuntime: pi
defaultPolicy: { id: pi }
afterRuntime: pi
pass: true

scenario: defaults PI repair does not leak into listed canonical Codex agent
beforeRuntime: { defaults: pi, regular: codex }
defaultPolicy: { id: pi }
listedPolicy: { id: codex }
afterRuntime: { defaults: pi, regular: codex }
pass: true

GREEN: all runtime pin repair scenarios preserved expected runtimes
```

- Observed result after fix: legacy `openai-codex/*` routes are rewritten to canonical `openai/*`, while explicit `pi` runtime pins remain effective for the repaired route. A listed agent that already explicitly uses canonical OpenAI with Codex routing keeps `codex` instead of inheriting the repaired defaults-level `pi` policy.
- What was not tested: full CI, a live PI OAuth login, and the separate `gpt-5.5` token-usage behavior discussed in #84038.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the same matrix on current `upstream/main` repaired all four scenarios to `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` and ended with `RED: one or more runtime pins were not preserved across doctor route repair`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: route repair synthesized a canonical `openai/*` Codex runtime policy before considering explicit pre-repair non-default runtime pins from legacy `openai-codex/*` model-map entries, provider-level policy, or provider catalog model policy.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered the default Codex repair path, but not the explicit non-Codex runtime-pin carriers or the listed-agent shield when defaults repair introduces a non-Codex runtime policy.
- Contributing context (if known): older route-repair behavior needed to preserve Codex auth routing after canonicalization, but that default should not override an explicit user runtime choice.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/doctor/shared/codex-route-warnings.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: model-map, provider-level, and provider-catalog runtime pins survive route repair; defaults-level preservation does not leak into listed canonical Codex agents.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: `maybeRepairCodexRoutes` plus the runtime resolver exercises the doctor repair output and the effective runtime selection without needing a live provider credential.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Codex route-repair tests covered canonical Codex behavior, not these explicit runtime-pin preservation cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor --fix` still canonicalizes legacy `openai-codex/*` route refs, but now preserves explicit non-default runtime pins instead of silently moving those repaired routes onto Codex runtime.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openai-codex/gpt-5.4 + explicit pi runtime -> doctor --fix -> openai/gpt-5.4 + codex runtime

After:
openai-codex/gpt-5.4 + explicit pi runtime -> doctor --fix -> openai/gpt-5.4 + pi runtime
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.1 local source checkout
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.4` repaired to `openai/gpt-5.4`
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): synthetic doctor config with explicit `agentRuntime.id = "pi"` in model-map, provider-level, and provider-catalog shapes

### Steps

1. Run the runtime-pin matrix against current `upstream/main` and observe RED.
2. Apply this patch.
3. Rerun the runtime-pin matrix and targeted doctor tests.

### Expected

- Repaired canonical `openai/gpt-5.4` routes preserve explicit `pi` runtime pins.
- Existing Codex route repair behavior remains green.

### Actual

- Matrix proof is GREEN after this patch.
- Targeted tests pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification commands run locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_REPO=/tmp/openclaw-84038-clean-20260521 pnpm exec tsx /tmp/repro-openclaw-84038-runtime-pin-matrix.mjs
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/codex-route-warnings.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor/shared/codex-route-warnings.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/codex-route-warnings.test.ts
git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD
```

Results:

```text
runtime-pin matrix: GREEN
codex-route-warnings.test.ts: 103 passed
repair-sequencing.test.ts: 10 passed
tsgo core: exit 0
oxfmt touched files: All matched files use the correct format.
git diff --check: passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: model-map runtime pin, provider-level runtime pin, provider-catalog model runtime pin, listed-agent shielding, existing Codex repair tests, repair sequencing tests, typecheck, formatting, and whitespace checks.
- Edge cases checked: `auto` / `default` runtime policy is not treated as an explicit pin; listed canonical agents keep Codex when defaults repair preserves PI; contributor PR avoids `CHANGELOG.md`.
- What you did **not** verify: full CI, live OAuth login, runtime token consumption, and maintainer-side ClawSweeper automerge.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No bot review conversations exist on this new PR at creation time.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving a non-Codex runtime pin during defaults repair could accidentally affect listed agents that explicitly use canonical OpenAI/Codex routing.
  - Mitigation: the patch shields explicitly listed canonical agents and adds a regression test/proof where defaults remain `pi` while the listed agent remains `codex`.
- Risk: provider-level and provider-catalog policy lookup could be broader than intended.
  - Mitigation: tests cover model-map precedence, provider-level pins, provider-catalog model pins, and existing repair sequencing remains green.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR is AI-assisted. I reviewed the diff and ran the verification listed above.

target.title: fix(codex): preserve responses session streams
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex PI runtime-auth session streams for `openai-codex/openai-codex-responses` could be re-routed through OpenClaw's boundary-aware Responses transport, bypassing the PI session stream that owns native continuation state.
- Solution: Preserve the exact Codex Responses PI session/custom stream and wrap it only to inject resolved run context (`apiKey`, `authProfileId`, `sessionId`, `signal`) and strip the cache-boundary marker.
- What changed: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.ts` now allowlists the exact Codex Responses session-stream shape before the generic runtime-auth boundary-aware branch; `stream-resolution.test.ts` locks in target and non-target routing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default Codex fallback still uses PI native transport, Cloudflare/runtime-auth custom streams still use boundary-aware transports, and PI native OpenAI-compatible provider streams still use boundary-aware transports.

## Motivation

- Related #84110.
- Local post-tool repros show the boundary-aware Codex PI route can replay a full-ish continuation after a real `exec` tool result, while preserving the PI session stream restores native cached-context delta behavior.
- This PR intentionally addresses the OpenClaw route-selection path observed locally. It does not claim exact reporter Docker/OrbStack/macOS/session parity without reporter or maintainer confirmation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #84110
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex PI `openai-codex/openai-codex-responses` post-tool continuation should stay on the PI session stream and reuse native cached-context delta state instead of falling back to OpenClaw boundary-aware full replay.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on this branch, Node 22, temporary OpenClaw state/config only, Codex PI sidecar with `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`, one real `exec` tool call. Real provider auth was injected into the temporary test state and restored afterward.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the local #84110 minimal PI post-tool harness from the source checkout. The prompt asked Codex to call `exec` exactly once with `printf '<token>\n'`, then answer with only the command output. Also parsed the resulting session JSONL to confirm the tool call/result boundary and token usage.
- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
branch: fix/84110-codex-session-streams
commit: 2ca5251adc fix: preserve codex responses session streams

context.compiled:
  streamStrategy=session-custom
  toolsLen=34

harness summary:
  exit=0
  provider=openai-codex
  model=gpt-5.4
  lastCallUsage={ input: 290, cacheRead: 16896, cacheWrite: 0, output: 20, total: 17206 }
  wsStats={ requests: 2, connectionsCreated: 1, connectionsReused: 1,
            fullContextRequests: 1, deltaRequests: 1,
            lastDeltaInputItems: 1, lastPreviousResponseId: resp_... }

session JSONL compact summary:
  assistant toolCall: name=exec, usage={ input: 17092, cacheRead: 0, output: 68 }
  toolResult: toolName=exec, status=completed, exitCode=0, isError=false
  assistant final: text=<expected token>, usage={ input: 290, cacheRead: 16896, output: 20 }

temp auth restore check:
  access=absent refresh=absent idToken=absent oauthRef=PRESENT
```

- Observed result after fix: The real post-tool continuation stayed on `session-custom`, reused the single PI connection (`connectionsReused=1`), sent one native delta request (`deltaRequests=1`, `lastDeltaInputItems=1`), and the final post-tool model call dropped to 290 input tokens with 16,896 cached tokens read.
- What was not tested: Exact reporter Docker/OrbStack/macOS/session transcript, maintainer/reporter environment confirmation, broad/full suite, and every provider. Non-target routing is covered by targeted unit tests below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Local unpatched v2026.5.18 control for the same minimal real `exec` shape used `streamStrategy=boundary-aware:openai-codex-responses`; the pre-tool call had `input=6478 cacheRead=10752`, and the post-tool final call had `input=17327 cacheRead=0`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The runtime-auth branch treated Codex Responses PI session/custom streams like other supported API custom streams and replaced them with OpenClaw's boundary-aware HTTP transport. For Codex PI sessions, that dropped the PI stream that owns native Responses continuation state.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no regression test for resolved-runtime-auth Codex Responses session streams where `currentStreamFn` is non-default/current-session state rather than the default PI stream.
- Contributing context (if known): The generic runtime-auth boundary-aware path is still required for other providers, including Cloudflare-style runtime auth. The fix therefore has to be an exact Codex Responses exception, not a broad rollback.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: A resolved-runtime-auth `openai-codex/openai-codex-responses` non-default session stream remains `session-custom`, is wrapped with `apiKey`, `authProfileId`, `sessionId`, and `signal`, strips `SYSTEM_PROMPT_CACHE_BOUNDARY`, and does not call `createBoundaryAwareStreamFnForModel`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: `stream-resolution.ts` is the route-selection seam where the stale/wrong transport was selected.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing tests covered default Codex fallback, Cloudflare/runtime-auth boundary-aware behavior, and PI native OpenAI-compatible boundary-aware behavior, but not this exact Codex session/custom stream shape.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex PI sessions using `openai-codex/openai-codex-responses` should preserve native session continuation behavior after tool calls instead of replaying the full-ish prompt through the boundary-aware transport for this runtime-auth session stream shape.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Codex PI session stream + resolved runtime auth
  -> generic boundary-aware transport branch
  -> boundary-aware:openai-codex-responses
  -> post-tool continuation can replay full-ish context

After:
Codex PI session stream + resolved runtime auth
  -> exact Codex Responses session-stream exception
  -> wrapped session-custom stream
  -> native PI delta continuation state is preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes --- the existing resolved runtime key is now injected into the preserved Codex session stream wrapper for this exact route, instead of into the boundary-aware transport. No new storage, logging, or credential lookup path is added.
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes --- this exact Codex PI route now uses the existing PI session stream rather than OpenClaw's boundary-aware Responses replay path.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The route is exact-match allowlisted to `provider=openai-codex` + `api=openai-codex-responses` + non-default session stream + resolved runtime key. Tests verify context stripping, `apiKey`/`authProfileId`/`sessionId`/`signal` injection, and that non-target runtime-auth routes still use boundary-aware transports.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local source checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22, repository test wrapper
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.4` through Codex PI sidecar
- Integration/channel (if any): PI embedded runner with real `exec` tool call
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary OpenClaw state/config; no real OpenClaw or Codex config was modified

### Steps

1. Run the targeted stream-resolution unit test.
2. Run the route-selection RED script that expects resolved-runtime-auth Codex Responses custom streams to report `session-custom`.
3. Run one minimal PI post-tool harness with a real `exec` tool call and parse the session JSONL + websocket stats.

### Expected

- Target route reports `session-custom`.
- Non-target runtime-auth custom streams still route boundary-aware.
- Real post-tool Codex continuation uses native delta reuse with small post-tool input and hot cache read.

### Actual

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.test.ts` passed 44/44 across `agents-core` and `agents-pi-embedded`.
- RED route script returned `{ "got": "session-custom", "expected": "session-custom" }`.
- Minimal real post-tool harness exited 0 with `streamStrategy=session-custom`, `deltaRequests=1`, `connectionsReused=1`, final `input=290`, and `cacheRead=16896`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Clean branch hygiene: one commit over `upstream/main`, two changed files only.
  - Diff whitespace: `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` passed.
  - Targeted unit tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/stream-resolution.test.ts` passed 44/44.
  - Route selection: RED script returned `session-custom` for resolved-runtime-auth Codex Responses custom stream.
  - Real behavior: minimal post-tool PI harness showed `streamStrategy=session-custom`, real `exec` tool call/result, and native delta reuse on the final answer.
  - Hygiene: temporary auth profile was restored (`access`, `refresh`, and `idToken` absent; `oauthRef` present), with no leftover #84110 runner or Codex app-server processes.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Default Codex fallback still goes through PI native transport.
  - Cloudflare/runtime-auth custom stream remains boundary-aware.
  - PI native OpenAI-compatible provider stream remains boundary-aware.
  - Wrapped target stream receives resolved `apiKey`, `authProfileId`, `sessionId`, and `signal`, and strips `SYSTEM_PROMPT_CACHE_BOUNDARY`.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Exact reporter Docker/OrbStack/macOS/session transcript.
  - Broad/full OpenClaw suite.
  - Every provider adapter.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

N/A --- no review conversations exist yet.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Preserving the Codex session stream could lose runtime auth/run-context injection.
  - Mitigation: The preserved stream is still wrapped; tests assert `apiKey`, `authProfileId`, `sessionId`, and `signal` are injected and cache-boundary marker stripping still happens.
- Risk: A generic runtime-auth transport fix could regress Cloudflare or OpenAI-compatible provider behavior.
  - Mitigation: The exception is exact-match allowlisted, and existing/new tests keep Cloudflare/runtime-auth and PI native OpenAI-compatible paths boundary-aware.
- Risk: This may not cover every reporter-specific #84110 condition.
  - Mitigation: PR wording uses `Related #84110` and documents that exact reporter environment parity was not tested; the patch is scoped to the OpenClaw route-selection failure proven locally.

## AI assistance

This investigation, local proof organization, and PR-body draft were prepared with AI assistance. The branch, diff, and verification outputs above were checked locally before submission.

target.title: fix(discord): fall back when voice adapter is unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord cron announce deliveries can fail with `discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable` when automatic TTS marks a text payload as voice audio.
- Solution: keep the Discord voice path first, but fall back to normal Discord text when the unavailable `discordVoice` adapter error occurs and visible text is present.
- What changed: preserve reply targeting across the fallback by passing both `replyTo` and `replyToId`, and teach the Discord text sender to accept `replyToId` as an alias.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no cron/core routing change; real Discord voice sends still run first; non-unavailable voice errors and media-only voice payloads still fail instead of being masked.

## Motivation

- Fixes message loss for text-only Discord cron announcements when automatic TTS selects a voice delivery path but the isolated cron context has no Discord voice outbound adapter.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84952
- Related #84952
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord `audioAsVoice` payload delivery no longer stops at `discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable` when visible text can be delivered through the normal Discord text path, and the fallback preserves the Discord reply target.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on PR head `c0a3c730369032e1b8cd2bd83ab4aa42f259773f`, sending through a real sandbox Discord text channel via the Discord API. The proof injects only the exact unavailable `discordVoice` error; the fallback `discord` text sender is not mocked and uses the real Discord outbound path. Channel id, bot token, and message ids are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /tmp/openclaw-84952-worktree.XdsWKl
export OPENCLAW_DISCORD_SMOKE_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted sandbox bot token>
export OPENCLAW_DISCORD_SMOKE_CHANNEL_ID=<redacted sandbox text channel id>
./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/pr84965/real-discord-fallback-proof.mts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from the real Discord outbound run and API readback, copied live output with ids redacted:

```json
{
  "proof": "openclaw-pr-84965-real-discord-outbound-fallback",
  "channel": "<redacted>",
  "nonce": "1779375505200-9b7d02",
  "voiceUnavailablePathAttempted": 1,
  "seedMessage": {
    "created": true,
    "id": "<redacted:seed>",
    "content": "OpenClaw PR #84965 proof seed 1779375505200-9b7d02"
  },
  "fallbackMessage": {
    "created": true,
    "id": "<redacted:fallback>",
    "content": "OpenClaw PR #84965 proof fallback visible text 1779375505200-9b7d02",
    "replyTargetMatchesSeed": true,
    "attachmentCount": 0
  },
  "result": {
    "channel": "discord",
    "messageId": "<redacted:fallback>",
    "channelId": "<redacted>"
  }
}
```

Independent Discord API readback of the latest channel messages filtered by the same nonce:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "nonce": "1779375505200-9b7d02",
  "matchingProofMessages": 2,
  "seedPresent": true,
  "fallbackPresent": true,
  "fallbackReplyTargetMatchesSeed": true,
  "fallbackAttachmentCount": 0,
  "ids": {
    "seed": "<redacted>",
    "fallback": "<redacted>"
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the unavailable voice-adapter path was attempted once, the fallback sent visible Discord text through the real Discord outbound path, Discord readback showed the fallback message replied to the intended seed message, and the fallback had `attachmentCount: 0` so the generated TTS media was not attached as normal media.
- What was not tested: a scheduled cron job was not run end-to-end in this sandbox; this proof exercises the Discord outbound `sendPayload` path with the exact unavailable `discordVoice` error and real Discord text delivery/readback. The existing regression test covers the cron-style `audioAsVoice` payload shape and reply-target propagation at the adapter seam.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```text
discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable.
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Discord payload delivery treats automatic TTS media as `audioAsVoice` and resolves `discordVoice`; isolated cron/default outbound deps can expose a lazy `discordVoice` sender even when no Discord voice adapter is registered, so delivery throws before normal Discord text delivery runs.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing Discord `audioAsVoice` coverage only covered the available voice helper path, not the unavailable lazy-adapter path with visible text fallback.
- Contributing context (if known): the fallback must preserve both Discord-native reply options and generic runtime reply options because the default channel sender reads `replyToId` / `replyToMessageId`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: an `audioAsVoice` payload with visible text and an unavailable `discordVoice` dep falls back to the Discord text sender, sends no generated voice media as regular media, and preserves `replyTo` plus `replyToId`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the Discord outbound plugin boundary and dependency contract, so this catches the failing path without changing cron/core routing.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord text announcements that were previously lost when the voice adapter was unavailable now deliver as normal Discord text when visible payload text exists.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord cron announce + auto TTS -> audioAsVoice -> unavailable discordVoice -> delivery fails

After:
Discord cron announce + auto TTS -> audioAsVoice -> unavailable discordVoice -> Discord text fallback -> delivered text
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord outbound plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): local test config only; no live Discord token used

### Steps

1. Reproduce the pre-fix failure shape with a Discord `audioAsVoice` payload and unavailable `discordVoice` sender.
2. Run the after-fix plugin-boundary proof script.
3. Run targeted Discord extension tests and the extensions type lane.

### Expected

- Visible text falls back to normal Discord text delivery when only the voice adapter is unavailable.
- The generated TTS media is not attached as regular media.
- Reply targeting is preserved.

### Actual

- The fallback text sender received the expected text, `replyTo`, `replyToId`, and no `mediaUrl`.
- Targeted Discord tests and type checks passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts` → 36 tests passed.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts` → 43 tests passed.
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo` → passed.
  - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` → clean.
  - `./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/openclaw-84952-after-fix-proof.mts` → produced the fallback JSON shown above.
- Edge cases checked: fallback only handles the known unavailable `discordVoice` adapter error; the code still rethrows non-unavailable voice errors and does not fall back for media-only voice payloads without visible text.
- What you did **not** verify: a real scheduled cron execution. The dedicated Real behavior proof above covers the outbound fallback through a real sandbox Discord text-channel send plus Discord API readback; GitHub checks ran on the PR branch.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a real Discord voice send error could be hidden by fallback.
  - Mitigation: the fallback catches only the known unavailable `discordVoice` adapter message; all other voice send errors are rethrown.
- Risk: fallback text could lose reply targeting in the generic sender path.
  - Mitigation: the fallback passes both `replyTo` and `replyToId`, and the Discord text sender accepts `replyToId` as an alias.

target.title: fix(agent): honor modelByChannel for ingress runs
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: ingress/requester agent runs for channel sessions ignored `channels.modelByChannel`, so announce-style runs could silently use the global agent default model.
- Solution: apply the channel model candidate before stored or explicit overrides, using the same alias-aware resolver as the regular model-selection path.
- What changed: added regression coverage for channel ingress selection, alias resolution, stored/explicit precedence, and auto-fallback primary probes.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): stored session overrides and explicit one-off provider/model overrides still win over channel defaults; no channel delivery, auth configuration/schema, credential storage, or external transport behavior is changed.

## Motivation

- Channel-level model routing can be displayed/configured while requester/announce ingress runs still execute on the agent default, which makes a healthy run use the wrong provider/model silently.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #60078
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Ingress/requester agent runs for a Discord channel with `channels.modelByChannel.discord.1489110116304424990 = anthropic/claude-opus-4-6` select the channel model, not the global default `openai/gpt-default`.

- Real environment tested:

Local Linux checkout on this branch, Node v22.22.1, real `openclaw gateway run` plus `gateway call agent`, temporary config/state, two local deterministic provider endpoints. The default-provider endpoint was configured to return `WRONG_PROVIDER_OPENAI_SELECTED` if it was contacted.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

1. Started a real local OpenClaw gateway with a temporary config and session state.
2. Seeded a Discord channel session for `discord/1489110116304424990`.
3. Configured `channels.modelByChannel.discord.1489110116304424990 = anthropic/claude-opus-4-6`.
4. Pointed the global default `openai/gpt-default` at a local endpoint that would return `WRONG_PROVIDER_OPENAI_SELECTED` if the old default-provider path was selected.
5. Ran `gateway call agent` for the seeded session and inspected provider request counts plus `agentMeta.provider/model`.

Local harness command used to run the proof:

```bash
bash /tmp/openclaw-60078-runtime-proof.sh
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
RUNTIME_PROOF_ROOT=/tmp/openclaw-60078-runtime-proof-YlpQ1r
gateway_port=39645
default_provider=openai/gpt-default (fake endpoint returns WRONG_PROVIDER_OPENAI_SELECTED if used)
channel_override=discord/1489110116304424990 -> anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
provider_request_summary={"anthropic_requests": 1, "openai_default_requests": 0, "anthropic_models": ["claude-opus-4-6"]}
agent_result_excerpt={
  "runId": "proof-60078-channel-model",
  "status": "ok",
  "summary": "completed",
  "result": {
    "payloads": [{ "text": "OK", "mediaUrl": null }],
    "meta": {
      "agentMeta": {
        "sessionId": "runtime-proof-session",
        "provider": "anthropic",
        "model": "claude-opus-4-6"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix:

The gateway run completed successfully through the channel-selected `anthropic/claude-opus-4-6` path. The default `openai/gpt-default` endpoint received zero requests, so the old wrong-provider path did not execute.

- What was not tested:

Live Discord API delivery and paid external provider APIs were not used; the tested behavior is the local gateway/provider routing contract with a seeded Discord channel session.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

The focused RED test on the old behavior failed with `AssertionError: expected 'ollama' to be 'anthropic'`, showing ingress selection used the default model instead of the channel model.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `agentCommandFromIngress` started from `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` and then applied stored/explicit overrides, but did not consider the existing `channels.modelByChannel` candidate for the session/channel context.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered direct user/session overrides but not the ingress path where a channel default should seed the initial provider/model.
- Contributing context (if known): neighboring status/config paths already knew about `modelByChannel`, which made the runtime selection gap easy to miss.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/agent.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: ingress runs use `modelByChannel` when no stored/explicit override exists; configured aliases resolve correctly; stored and explicit overrides still win; auto-fallback primary probes use the channel primary.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the command ingress selection point directly while preserving existing override precedence contracts.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for the channel ingress selection gap.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Channel sessions with `channels.modelByChannel` now use that configured channel model for ingress/requester agent runs unless a stored session override or explicit one-off override is present.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
channel ingress -> agent default model -> stored/explicit overrides

After:
channel ingress -> agent default model -> channel model candidate -> stored/explicit overrides -> selected provider/model
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local checkout, Node v22.22.1
- Model/provider: local deterministic provider endpoints for the `openai/gpt-default` and `anthropic/claude-opus-4-6` provider-id paths
- Integration/channel (if any): seeded Discord channel session
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.modelByChannel.discord.1489110116304424990 = anthropic/claude-opus-4-6`, global default `openai/gpt-default`

### Steps

1. Seed a Discord channel session in a temporary session store.
2. Start `openclaw gateway run` with temporary config/state and local provider endpoints.
3. Run `gateway call agent` for the seeded session and inspect provider request counts plus `agentMeta.provider/model`.

### Expected

- The channel-selected provider/model is used.
- The global default provider endpoint is not contacted.

### Actual

- `anthropic_requests=1`, `openai_default_requests=0`.
- `agentMeta.provider=anthropic`, `agentMeta.model=claude-opus-4-6`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - focused regression subset: modelByChannel alias, auto-fallback, stored precedence, explicit precedence
  - full `src/commands/agent.test.ts`: 27 passed
  - post-fix runtime proof: channel provider selected, default provider not contacted
- Edge cases checked:
  - configured model aliases resolve for channel entries
  - stored session model overrides still beat channel defaults
  - explicit one-off run overrides still beat channel defaults
  - auto-fallback primary probe uses the channel primary
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live Discord API delivery
  - paid external provider APIs
  - full repository CI locally

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No existing PR review conversations yet.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: channel defaults could accidentally override stronger per-session or one-off user choices.
  - Mitigation: tests lock stored and explicit override precedence ahead of channel defaults.
- Risk: bare channel model aliases could bypass canonical model selection behavior.
  - Mitigation: channel entries resolve through the shared alias-aware resolver, with a regression test for a configured alias.
- Risk: once ingress runs honor `modelByChannel`, existing sessions can expose a separate auth/session-state edge when a stored `authProfileOverride` belongs to a different provider than the channel-selected model.
  - Mitigation: this PR keeps the routing fix focused and does not broaden auth-profile persistence policy. A narrow follow-up patch with regression coverage is prepared to handle that route-local persistence edge separately, so reviewers can evaluate the documented channel-routing fix independently.

target.title: fix(prompt-cache): preserve prefix across hook/runtime context
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #85203 surfaced a real prompt-cache hit-rate problem: volatile system-level context can change bytes in the cacheable system-prompt prefix, causing turn 2+ cache misses. The subtle contract boundary is that `prependSystemContext` / `appendSystemContext` are documented static-guidance fields and should remain cacheable; volatile system-level text needs an explicit dynamic path below the cache-boundary marker.
- Solution: keep existing static hook system context in the cacheable prefix, add and wire `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` for volatile system-level text, and make all prompt-builder consumers handle the dynamic fields consistently. Existing override/runtime-extra cache-boundary handling and marker-substring stripping remain preserved.
- Latest remediation: commit `935bb04a2d` wires the new dynamic fields through the CLI prepare path and SDK-exported harness compaction helper, fixes `appendSystemContext` placement for marker-bearing base prompts, moves active image/video/music task hints to the dynamic prepend slot, and updates public docs for the static-vs-dynamic hook contract.
- Scope boundary: sites from #85203 that operate on user-turn text or inbound message bodies remain out of scope. Existing static plugin guidance remains cacheable; this PR does not migrate static callers below the marker.

## Motivation

The cache-boundary marker exists so the system-prompt stable prefix stays byte-identical across turns. Providers that cache prompt prefixes can then write the prefix once and read it on later turns. If per-turn state lands above the marker, the prefix changes on every turn and the cache misses repeatedly.

The correct contract is split by volatility:

- static system guidance: `prependSystemContext` / `appendSystemContext` stay above the marker and are cacheable;
- volatile system-level context: `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` lands below the marker;
- volatile user-turn context: `prependContext` / `appendContext` stays in the per-turn prompt path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #85203
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

The issue is not auto-closed because the issue body enumerates multiple dist:line sites across different surfaces. This PR fixes the system-prompt cache-boundary surfaces and leaves final issue closure to maintainers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Volatile system-level context now stays below the cache-boundary marker while static hook system guidance stays above it. The proof composes a marker-bearing base system prompt with constant static prepend/append context and varying per-turn dynamic prepend/append context. It asserts that the bytes above the marker are identical across all turns, the dynamic bytes do not leak into that stable prefix, and live provider cache counters show turn 2+ cache reads.

- Real environment tested:

Standalone TypeScript proof script (`/tmp/proof-cache-current.ts`) run from the PR worktree imports the current source via `tsx`, composes the system prompt through `composeSystemPromptWithHookContext`, then sends a 3-turn sequence to two live cache-eligible provider APIs. The script writes `/tmp/proof-cache-current-results.json` and prints the cache counters copied below.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
cd /tmp/openclaw-85203/work
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-cache-current.ts
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture, copied live output):

Stable-prefix and suffix assertions from `/tmp/proof-cache-current-results.json`:

```
stable_prefix_byte_identical_across_turns = true
stable_prefix_lengths = [28207, 28207, 28207]
dynamic_suffix_hashes = [
  "2b39ebd841f2805fae8c4745b9073a0c862443168cc001a8b24ea45698761ab8",
  "b484d46d4096548d1a612b69fd2987e5bf7737f366bd59ef8498eb5a94809fbf",
  "a4013eed6036dc7513f4281574de7d42c2fcd488a7ab939abff09b9b3a16592f"
]
static_append_in_stable_prefix = true
dynamic_context_in_stable_prefix = false
```

Provider A response usage:

```
turn 1   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=5748  cache_read_input_tokens=0
turn 2   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=0     cache_read_input_tokens=5748
turn 3   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=0     cache_read_input_tokens=5748
```

Provider B response usage:

```
turn 1   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=0
turn 2   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=5248
turn 3   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=5248
```

- Observed result after fix:

The stable prefix stayed byte-identical across all 3 turns even though the dynamic prepend/append context changed every turn. Provider A created the prefix cache on turn 1 and read the same prefix on turns 2 and 3. Provider B reported cached prefix tokens on turns 2 and 3. Static append context remained in the cacheable prefix; dynamic context stayed out of it.

- What was not tested:
    - Long-TTL cache mode; the 3-turn proof used the default short cache window.
    - Managed server-side conversation-cache paths that do not use prompt-prefix cache semantics.
    - Full-suite execution; focused affected tests and `check:changed` were used because unrelated baseline flakes exist outside this change.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: volatile system-level context and static hook guidance did not have distinct public hook result fields. That made it easy for per-turn state to travel through a cacheable system-context path or for a consumer backend to silently drop newly added dynamic fields.
- Secondary bug: `appendSystemContext` used flat append semantics, so marker-bearing base prompts could place the static append below the marker even though the field is documented as cacheable static guidance.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered helper behavior and one runner path but not the full consumer matrix for the public hook result type. The CLI prepare path and SDK-exported harness compaction helper were not initially exercising the dynamic fields.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [x] Unit test
    - [x] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
    - `src/agents/system-prompt-cache-boundary.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-system-prompt.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/harness/prompt-compaction-hook-helpers.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in:
    - static hook system context remains above the marker and cacheable;
    - dynamic hook system context lands below the marker;
    - marker-bearing `appendSystemContext` stays above the marker;
    - active media task hints are dynamic, not static prefix content;
    - CLI and harness prompt builders both preserve the dynamic fields;
    - marker substrings inside additions are stripped before downstream split/strip logic can be confused.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests verify the byte placement and consumer propagation directly without requiring network calls; the live proof is kept as external evidence rather than the primary regression guard.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

1. Existing static hook system fields remain cacheable and should be used only for stable guidance. This preserves the documented contract for static plugin guidance.
2. New dynamic hook system fields are available for volatile system-level text and are routed below the cache-boundary marker.
3. Active image/video/music task hints now travel through the dynamic system-context path instead of the static prepend path, so they no longer perturb the cacheable prefix when task status changes.
4. `appendSystemContext` for marker-bearing base prompts is inserted before the marker, matching its cacheable-static contract.
5. Runtime extra system prompt additions in the override path are assembled through the cache-boundary helpers; marker substrings inside additions are stripped before provider submission.

## Diagram

```text
Final shape:

  static prepend
  stable base prompt
  static append
  ╎ CACHE BOUNDARY ╎
  dynamic prepend
  existing dynamic suffix
  dynamic append

The provider sees the marker stripped before submission, but the split point keeps
cacheable prefix bytes stable while dynamic bytes vary below the boundary.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

Marker-substring sanitization in cache-boundary helper additions is defense-in-depth: a hook or runtime-extra value that contains the sentinel cannot smuggle an extra split marker into provider-visible text.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.1, pnpm
- Model/provider: provider-agnostic prompt assembly; live proof used two cache-eligible provider APIs.
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A (system-prompt assembly path).
- Relevant config (redacted): default short cache window; provider credentials supplied through local environment/files and not printed.

### Steps

1. Construct a deterministic marker-bearing base system prompt with a large stable prefix.
2. Compose 3 turns through `composeSystemPromptWithHookContext` with constant static prepend/append context and varying dynamic prepend/append context.
3. Assert prefix hashes and lengths are identical across turns while dynamic suffix hashes vary.
4. Send each turn to two live cache-eligible provider APIs.
5. Capture provider cache counters per turn.

### Expected

- Stable prefix bytes remain identical across all 3 turns.
- Dynamic context does not appear above the marker.
- Static append context appears above the marker.
- Turn 2+ provider responses report cache reads / cached prefix tokens.

### Actual

Matches Expected — see the Real behavior proof section above for full numbers.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused tests passed: 8 files / 380 tests across the embedded runner, CLI prepare, prompt helpers, and harness prompt-compaction paths. Affected extension harness test passed: 204 tests. `git diff --check` and path-scoped `pnpm check:changed -- <11 touched paths>` passed with lanes `core, coreTests, docs`.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
    - Ran the current-head live proof script and observed stable-prefix byte equality plus turn 2+ cache counters on both provider APIs.
    - Confirmed `appendSystemContext` stays above the marker for marker-bearing base prompts.
    - Confirmed CLI prepare and harness prompt-compaction paths preserve the dynamic fields.
    - Confirmed active media task hints are resolved into dynamic system context for user/manual triggers and skipped for non-user triggers.
    - Ran focused affected tests, affected extension harness test, `git diff --check`, and path-scoped `pnpm check:changed`.
- Edge cases checked:
    - Marker-bearing base prompt with static append.
    - Static and dynamic hook fields set together.
    - Dynamic prepend and dynamic append ordering around existing dynamic suffix.
    - Active media task hints with user/manual vs non-user triggers.
    - Harness helper merging prompt-build and legacy hook results before shared composition.
- What you did **not** verify:
    - Full-suite execution; unrelated baseline flakes exist outside this change.
    - Long-TTL cache mode.
    - Managed server-side conversation-cache provider paths.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

The PR remains draft until the remediation commit and updated proof are reviewed.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` for existing static hook system-context callers. Additive dynamic fields are optional.
- Config/env changes? `No`.
- Migration needed? `No` for static guidance. Plugins that currently pass volatile system-level text through static system-context fields should move that text to `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` to preserve prompt-cache hits.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a consumer backend silently drops one of the additive dynamic hook fields.
    - Mitigation: embedded, CLI, and SDK-exported harness prompt builders are all wired and covered by tests.
- Risk: `appendSystemContext` placement changes visible ordering for marker-bearing base prompts.
    - Mitigation: the field is documented as cacheable static guidance; the new placement matches that contract and is covered by a marker-bearing test.
- Risk: active media task hints moving below the marker changes where the model sees those hints in the final stripped prompt.
    - Mitigation: the content remains system-level text and now follows the volatility contract; tests cover inclusion and trigger gating.

---

AI-assisted; contributor manually reviewed and is responsible for the change.

target.title: fix(doctor): recover sibling moltbot config
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #54200 by adding a narrow recovery path for the upgrade layout where `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` is missing or skeletal while the user's real legacy config still exists at `~/.openclaw/moltbot.json`.

- `doctor` preflight copies sibling `moltbot.json` into canonical `openclaw.json` when the canonical file is missing.
- If canonical `openclaw.json` exists but is skeletal, read-only doctor now only warns; `openclaw doctor --fix` backs it up and restores from sibling `moltbot.json`.
- The replacement path copies through a temp file, restores the original canonical config on failure, and chmods the recovered canonical config to `0600`.
- Missing canonical config recovery now installs with no-overwrite semantics, so a concurrently-created canonical `openclaw.json` is preserved.
- Gateway startup adds a targeted hint when `gateway.mode` is missing and the sibling legacy config exists.

This does **not** add `moltbot.json` back to the normal config resolver or public config candidate list.

## Verification

2026-05-24 update, head `6be3f29935afda151365d42df45399357e239eaf`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 2 files / 36 tests passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts`
- `git diff --check refs/remotes/origin/main...HEAD`

Earlier verification:

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts --reporter=dot` (2 files, 35 tests passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/paths.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-preflight.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts --reporter=dot` (4 files, 92 tests passed)
- `git diff --check refs/remotes/origin/main...HEAD`

`pnpm check:changed` reaches an unrelated current-main core-test typecheck failure in `scripts/control-ui-i18n.ts` for missing declarations on `./npm-runner.mjs`, `./pnpm-runner.mjs`, and `./windows-cmd-helpers.mjs`. The core production typecheck lane passed before that failure.

## Real behavior proof

Real temp home: `/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV` with:

- skeletal canonical config: `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` containing only `update.channel`
- sibling legacy config: `~/.openclaw/moltbot.json` containing `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.port=19091`, and `agents.defaults.model`
- legacy file mode before repair: `0644`

Commands run from this branch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --no-workspace-suggestions --non-interactive
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node --import tsx src/entry.ts doctor --no-workspace-suggestions --non-interactive --fix
```

Observed after read-only doctor:

- Output included: `Found legacy sibling config at /tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV/.openclaw/moltbot.json; run openclaw doctor --fix to recover it into /tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV/.openclaw/openclaw.json.`
- `openclaw.json` remained the original skeletal file.
- No `openclaw.json.pre-moltbot-migration.*` backup was created by read-only doctor.

Observed after `doctor --fix`:

- Output included `Backed up skeletal config` and `Migrated legacy config` for `moltbot.json -> openclaw.json`.
- `openclaw.json` contained recovered `gateway.mode: "local"`, `gateway.port: 19091`, and `agents.defaults.model: "openai/gpt-5.5"`.
- Backup existed: `openclaw.json.pre-moltbot-migration.2026-05-24T13-43-20-209Z`.
- Final canonical config permissions: `mode=600 path=/tmp/openclaw-54200-proof-2ODsvV/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.

## Additional Safety Coverage

The latest update adds focused coverage for the remaining ClawSweeper findings:

- missing-canonical recovery preserves a canonical `openclaw.json` created after the initial existence check, using no-overwrite installation semantics
- gateway hint tests use a per-test mocked state directory instead of a fixed `/tmp/moltbot.json` fixture

target.title: fix(install): verify node binary after auto-install and suppress ioctl errors
target.body:
## Problem

The Node.js auto-installer reports "Node.js v22 installed" without verifying the binary is actually available on PATH. On WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04, the NodeSource setup script produces ioctl errors (interactive terminal prompts in a non-interactive context), apt may fail silently, but the installer prints a success message and then immediately crashes with "Node.js v22+ is required."

Two root causes:
1. The NodeSource setup script (`bash "$tmp"`) runs without `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive`, so it attempts interactive terminal operations that produce ioctl errors on WSL2
2. The installer prints `ui_success "Node.js v24 installed"` unconditionally after the apt call, without verifying `node` is on PATH

## Fix

1. Pass `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` to the NodeSource setup script execution (both root and sudo paths)
2. Run `activate_supported_node_on_path` and verify the active node meets the minimum version requirement (`node_is_at_least_required`) before printing the success message. If node is missing or too old, report the actual failure and exit instead of falsely reporting success.

Fixes #73837

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** The Node.js auto-installer now (1) passes `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` via `sudo -E env` to suppress ioctl errors on WSL2/Linux, and (2) verifies the active node binary meets the minimum version after package installation before reporting success. A failed or incomplete install now exits with a clear error instead of falsely reporting "Node.js installed."

**Real environment tested:** Clean Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS Docker container (no Node.js preinstalled), and macOS 15.5 (Darwin 25.5.0), Node v26.0.0, bash 5.3.9, openclaw dev checkout at `a65f332744`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1. Ran the patched `node_is_at_least_required` guard on a **clean Ubuntu 24.04 container with no Node.js installed**, sourcing the functions directly from the patched `scripts/install.sh`:

```console
$ docker run --rm -v ./scripts/install.sh:/tmp/install.sh:ro ubuntu:24.04 bash -c '
  echo "Container: $(cat /etc/os-release | grep PRETTY_NAME | cut -d= -f2)"
  echo "Node.js: $(which node 2>/dev/null || echo "NOT INSTALLED")"
  source <(sed -n "/^NODE_MIN_MAJOR=/,/^NODE_MIN_VERSION=/p" /tmp/install.sh)
  source <(sed -n "/^parse_node_version_components_for_binary()/,/^}/p" /tmp/install.sh)
  source <(sed -n "/^parse_node_version_components()/,/^}/p" /tmp/install.sh)
  source <(sed -n "/^node_is_at_least_required()/,/^}/p" /tmp/install.sh)
  echo "Minimum required: v${NODE_MIN_VERSION}"
  echo ""
  echo "--- No node (clean container) ---"
  node_is_at_least_required 2>/dev/null && echo "FAIL" || echo "PASS: rejected"
  mkdir -p /tmp/fn
  echo "--- v18.0.0 (old LTS) ---"
  printf "#!/bin/bash\necho v18.0.0" > /tmp/fn/node; chmod +x /tmp/fn/node
  PATH="/tmp/fn" node_is_at_least_required 2>/dev/null && echo "FAIL" || echo "PASS: rejected v18.0.0"
  echo "--- v22.18.0 (one minor below minimum) ---"
  printf "#!/bin/bash\necho v22.18.0" > /tmp/fn/node
  PATH="/tmp/fn" node_is_at_least_required 2>/dev/null && echo "FAIL" || echo "PASS: rejected v22.18.0"
  echo "--- v22.19.0 (exactly minimum) ---"
  printf "#!/bin/bash\necho v22.19.0" > /tmp/fn/node
  PATH="/tmp/fn" node_is_at_least_required 2>/dev/null && echo "PASS: accepted v22.19.0" || echo "FAIL"
  echo "--- v24.15.0 (recommended) ---"
  printf "#!/bin/bash\necho v24.15.0" > /tmp/fn/node
  PATH="/tmp/fn" node_is_at_least_required 2>/dev/null && echo "PASS: accepted v24.15.0" || echo "FAIL"
'
Container: "Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS"
Node.js: NOT INSTALLED
Minimum required: v22.19

--- No node (clean container) ---
PASS: rejected
--- v18.0.0 (old LTS) ---
PASS: rejected v18.0.0
--- v22.18.0 (one minor below minimum) ---
PASS: rejected v22.18.0
--- v22.19.0 (exactly minimum) ---
PASS: accepted v22.19.0
--- v24.15.0 (recommended) ---
PASS: accepted v24.15.0
```

Step 2. Verified `DEBIAN_FRONTEND` propagation in both root and sudo NodeSource setup paths:

```console
$ grep -n "DEBIAN_FRONTEND.*bash.*tmp\|sudo.*DEBIAN_FRONTEND.*bash" scripts/install.sh
1643:                run_quiet_step "Configuring NodeSource repository" env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive bash "$tmp"
1646:                run_quiet_step "Configuring NodeSource repository" sudo -E env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive bash "$tmp"
```

Step 3. Verified post-install guard ordering (guard runs BEFORE success message):

```console
$ awk '/node_is_at_least_required/{print NR": "$0}' scripts/install.sh | head -5
1340: node_is_at_least_required() {
1523:     if node_is_at_least_required; then
1581:         if node_is_at_least_required; then
1678:         if ! node_is_at_least_required; then
$ awk '/ui_success.*Node.js.*installed/{print NR": "$0}' scripts/install.sh
1611:         ui_success "Node.js installed"
1632:             ui_success "Node.js v${NODE_DEFAULT_MAJOR} installed"
1685:         ui_success "Node.js v${NODE_DEFAULT_MAJOR} installed"
```

The `node_is_at_least_required` guard at line 1678 runs before the success message at line 1685, so a failed or incomplete apt install now exits with a clear error instead of falsely reporting success.

**Evidence after fix:** On a clean Ubuntu 24.04 container with no Node preinstalled, the patched guard correctly rejects missing node, old node (v18.0.0), boundary-below node (v22.18.0), and correctly accepts exact-minimum (v22.19.0) and recommended (v24.15.0). The `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` flag is confirmed in both root and sudo apt paths. Guard ordering shows verification runs before the success message.

**Observed result after fix:** All 5 guard scenarios produce correct accept/reject decisions on clean Ubuntu 24.04. The root path uses `env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive bash "$tmp"` and the sudo path uses `sudo -E env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive bash "$tmp"`, preserving the caller environment while suppressing interactive prompts.

**What was not tested:** Full end-to-end `curl | bash` installer run that triggers the actual `apt-get install nodejs` call from NodeSource. The guard functions and env var propagation are verified on real Linux (Ubuntu 24.04 Docker) and macOS. The actual apt package download was not exercised because it requires NodeSource repository credentials and network access that vary by environment.

target.title: fix(codex): preserve semantic native threads across compaction
target.body:
## Summary

This is PR #4 in the Codex native-thread stack, after #85978, #86069, and #86094. It turns the diagnostics foundation into the long-term cache/lifecycle fix for compatible context-engine `thread_bootstrap` bindings:

- preserve Codex `thread_bootstrap` bindings across successful context-engine-owned compaction;
- copy preserved bindings to successor session files during transcript rollover, then clear the archived/original binding;
- reread the active Codex binding after forced compaction before rebuilding the Codex prompt;
- if optimistic semantic reuse rotates during startup, rebuild the prompt for the fresh thread before `turn/start` so the fresh thread receives projected context;
- keep legacy, ownerless, non-bootstrap, mismatch, and app-server rejection paths rotating/clearing as before;
- hash sensitive MCP/environment comparison tokens before persisting or emitting lifecycle diagnostics;
- add `context-engine-compaction-preserved-binding` lifecycle diagnostics and docs explaining the preserved vs invalidated paths.

## Why this is not “raise 70k to 258k/272k”

The default `70000` native reuse guard remains a legacy/stale-binding safety rail. The healthy Codex + LCM path is semantic ownership:

1. Context engine returns `contextProjection.mode = "thread_bootstrap"` with a stable epoch/fingerprint.
2. OpenClaw injects large assembled context once into the Codex native thread.
3. Later turns resume that native thread and send only the current prompt when the engine/policy/projection/tool identity still matches.
4. Compaction or rollover preserves the binding only when that semantic identity remains safe.
5. Actual app-server overflow/rejection still clears the binding before fresh-thread retry.

The important distinction is model capacity vs warm-thread lifecycle policy:

| Value | Meaning | Share of 70k guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `70000` | OpenClaw proactive native-thread reuse guard for legacy/stale bindings | 100% |
| `272000` | OpenClaw Codex runtime input cap used for GPT-5.5 forward-compat rows | 70k is 25.7% |
| `400000` | GPT-5.5 native/context window in current OpenClaw catalog/test fixtures | 70k is 17.5% |

With a 30% post-compaction target, even a compacted transcript can land above the guard:

- 30% of 258k = 77.4k
- 30% of 272k = 81.6k

So repeatedly applying a hard 70k warm-thread rotation rule to otherwise compatible Codex native threads makes long-running sessions take the cold path right after compaction. This PR keeps the 70k guard for legacy/non-semantic bindings, but lets semantically-owned `thread_bootstrap` bindings survive compaction/rollover.

## Local bootstrap size scan

I ran a bounded local scan over named bootstrap candidates (`AGENTS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `BOOTSTRAP.md`) under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos`, `/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex`, `~/.openclaw`, and `~/.codex`, using `ceil(bytes / 4)` as a conservative token estimate.

| Metric | Result |
| --- | ---: |
| Candidate files | 3,235 |
| Aggregate directories | 2,032 |
| Aggregate p50 | ~637 tokens |
| Aggregate p90 | ~3.8k tokens |
| Aggregate p99 | ~13.5k tokens |
| Max aggregate | ~117.7k tokens (`~/.codex/memories/MEMORY.md`) |
| Largest repeated AGENTS candidate | ~23.6k tokens |

Interpretation: the bug is not “every AGENTS.md is over 70k.” The failure mode is total rendered pressure: compacted history + context-engine projection + workspace bootstrap/developer instructions + native rollout growth. Once that total has created a warm native thread, the efficient Codex path is to preserve the semantically valid native thread rather than reproject the large stable payload every turn.

## Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Long-running Codex session"] --> B{"Saved native binding?"}
  B -- "Legacy / ownerless / non-bootstrap" --> C{"session/native tokens >= guard?"}
  C -- "yes" --> D["Rotate cold: thread/start + reproject context"]
  C -- "no" --> E["Try normal resume"]
  B -- "context-engine thread_bootstrap" --> F{"Engine id + policy + epoch/fingerprint + tools match?"}
  F -- "no" --> D
  F -- "yes" --> G["Resume warm native thread"]
  G --> H["Send current prompt only"]
  H --> I{"Context-engine compaction?"}
  I -- "same file, identity stable" --> J["Preserve binding in place"]
  I -- "successor rollover, identity stable" --> K["Copy binding to successor; clear archived original"]
  I -- "identity changes or app-server rejects" --> D
  J --> G
  K --> G
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Compatible Codex context-engine `thread_bootstrap` native bindings are preserved across successful context-engine compaction and successor session-file rollover, while legacy/non-bootstrap, mismatch, and app-server rejection paths still rotate/clear.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw worktree on the Lexar-backed checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-semantic-reuse-guard`, branch `codex/semantic-native-thread-preservation`, current head `8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad`. The proof used temp session files under `/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-pr-86160-proof-8b405b4d5c` and the actual exported `reconcileContextEngineCompactedCodexBinding(...)`, `writeCodexAppServerBinding(...)`, `readCodexAppServerBinding(...)`, `clearCodexAppServerBinding(...)`, and `startOrResumeThread(...)` helpers from this checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a one-off `pnpm exec tsx` proof probe, not Vitest/tsgo/build. The probe wrote matching `thread_bootstrap` bindings, simulated a compactor clearing the sidecar before same-file compaction, simulated successor rollover copy/archived clear, and seeded a legacy raw user-MCP binding to prove the mismatch path rotates through `thread/start` and persists a hashed comparison token. I also verified the patched worktree identity and whitespace state with `pwd`, `git rev-parse HEAD`, `git branch --show-current`, `git status --porcelain`, and `git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD`.
- Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from the current patched worktree proof probe:

```text
[agent/embedded] context-engine-owned Codex app-server compaction preserved native thread-bootstrap binding
[agent/embedded] context-engine-owned Codex app-server compaction preserved native thread-bootstrap binding
{
  "head": "8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad",
  "proofDir": "/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-pr-86160-proof-8b405b4d5c",
  "sameFileThreadBootstrap": {
    "outcome": {
      "invalidated": false,
      "preserved": true,
      "successorInvalidated": false
    },
    "restoredAfterCompactorClearedSidecar": true,
    "preservedThreadId": "thread-same-file-warm",
    "projectionMode": "thread_bootstrap",
    "projectionEpoch": "epoch-proof",
    "projectionFingerprint": "projection-fp-proof"
  },
  "successorRolloverThreadBootstrap": {
    "outcome": {
      "invalidated": false,
      "preserved": true,
      "successorInvalidated": false
    },
    "archivedBindingPresent": false,
    "successorThreadId": "thread-rollover-warm",
    "successorSessionFileBasename": "rollover-successor.jsonl",
    "projectionMode": "thread_bootstrap",
    "projectionEpoch": "epoch-rollover",
    "projectionFingerprint": "projection-fp-rollover"
  },
  "mcpConfigMismatchRotation": {
    "lifecycleAction": "started",
    "methods": [
      "thread/start"
    ],
    "previousThreadRotated": true,
    "savedThreadId": "thread-mcp-new",
    "userMcpServersFingerprintPrefix": "sha256:",
    "leakedMcpSecretInBinding": false,
    "leakedMcpPathInBinding": false
  }
}
$ pwd
/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-semantic-reuse-guard
$ git rev-parse HEAD
8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad
$ git branch --show-current
codex/semantic-native-thread-preservation
$ git status --porcelain
$ git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD
```

`git status --porcelain` and `git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD` produced no output.

- Observed result after fix: Same-file compaction returned `preserved: true` and restored `thread-same-file-warm` after the sidecar was cleared. Successor rollover returned `preserved: true`, cleared the archived/original binding (`archivedBindingPresent: false`), and copied `thread-rollover-warm` with the same `thread_bootstrap` epoch/fingerprint to the successor session file. The MCP mismatch path did not resume the stale thread; it called `thread/start`, saved `thread-mcp-new`, persisted a `sha256:` comparison fingerprint, and did not leak the seeded MCP email/path into the saved binding.
- What was not tested: No local Vitest/tsgo suite, package build, or live Gateway/Discord/Codex app-server replay was run locally, to avoid the local resource issue called out in AGENTS.md. GitHub Actions remains the validation gate for type, package-boundary, and test execution.
- Before evidence: The motivating production-style logs reported repeated `codex app-server native transcript exceeded active token limit; starting a fresh thread` at native token counts above the 70k reuse guard; issue #86023 and this PR body include the budget math and local bootstrap-size scan explaining that failure mode.

## Validation

Local validation intentionally skipped heavy suites per AGENTS.md / user instruction. GitHub Actions is the validation gate.

Local:

- one-off `pnpm exec tsx` lifecycle proof probe, as captured above
- `git diff --check HEAD^ HEAD`

GitHub Actions:

- current head `8b405b4d5c59432b4f3218b570609d3d43f5a4ad` is green, including the previously failing logging support-export slice after the stability-bundle `mode` parser fix and the rerun after the unrelated model-catalog timeout.

Not run locally:

- Vitest focused slices
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- live Gateway/Discord/Codex app-server replay

## Stack / Review Notes

This PR is stacked on #86094. The diff to review is the top commit:

- `8b405b4d5c fix(codex): preserve semantic native threads across compaction`

Refs #86023.
